text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 14 October 2019 

Dr Helen Morris  
Riverside Medical Practice  
22 Wharf Road, London N1 7SP  

Dear Dr Helen Morris,  

Emily Carter, DOB: 04-11-1989, Hospital No: U284613 NHS No. 6012847392  
Flat 3, 14 Kingsley Court, London W2 5QL 

Summary/Diagnoses: Generalised epilepsy (predominantly nocturnal events)/nocturnal motor events per partner report/anxiety symptoms under primary care review/non-smoker/occasional alcohol use. 

Medication: Sodium valproate 250 mg in the morning and 500 mg at night. 
Vitamin D3 800 IU once daily. 
Folic acid 5 mg once daily (pre-conception counselling discussed previously; patient currently not planning pregnancy).  

I reviewed Ms Emily Carter in clinic today. She attended alone; her partner was not present at today’s review. Background as per prior notes and today’s account: events are predominantly nocturnal per partner reports. The patient herself recalls awakening unrefreshed with myalgia and tongue soreness on several occasions. She has no clear daytime events, no witnessed absences or myoclonic jerks while awake, and no known triggers beyond poor sleep. There is no family history of epilepsy. She does not drive. 

Seizures: She first experienced a seizure in July 2019 while living in Australia. It occurred during sleep, with sudden jerks of the left leg. Her next seizure came in October 2019 the same year in New Zealand, once more from sleep, lasting three minutes and showing the same semiology. These descriptions align with her overall history of predominantly nocturnal events reported by her partner, though the partner was not present today to corroborate further detail. Since starting sodium valproate two weeks ago after initial neurology triage, she has had no further nocturnal episodes or post-event soreness. She is keeping a sleep and seizure diary. 

Examination: Alert and oriented, speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Motor exam normal tone, power 5/5 throughout, no pronator drift. Reflexes symmetric, plantar flexor bilaterally. Coordination normal; gait normal. No tongue lacerations today; no focal neurological deficit. 

Investigations: Routine MRI brain (reported last week) normal. Screening bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile) within reference ranges. Valproate level not yet performed (commenced recently). EEG booked for next week; she describes photic sensitivity as unlikely but will proceed with routine and sleep-deprived protocols if feasible. 

Assessment: Clinical history is most consistent with generalised epilepsy with predominantly nocturnal manifestations. Given the semiology, lack of focal neurological signs, normal MRI, and improvement since initiating valproate, a generalised epilepsy syndrome remains most likely. We will review EEG findings to support this. 

Plan: 
- Continue sodium valproate 250 mg AM and 500 mg PM; reassess after EEG. 
- Check valproate trough level and LFTs in 2 weeks via GP practice; please code for antiepileptic drug monitoring. 
- Safety counselling provided: nocturnal precautions, supervision advice, and SUDEP discussion in line with NICE guidance. 
- Seizure and sleep diary to continue; partner to attend next appointment if possible for collateral history. 
- DVLA: patient currently not driving; advised to remain off driving and to follow DVLA guidance should circumstances change. 
- Follow-up in 8–10 weeks in epilepsy clinic or earlier if further events occur. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes two seizures: first in July 2019 and next in October 2019. The clinic date is 14 October 2019. The October event is in the same month as the clinic visit; July to October yields 2 seizures over roughly 3 months. Since starting valproate two weeks ago she has had no further episodes, but this is far less than 6 months, so she cannot be considered seizure free. No other frequencies are specified. Per the rule to compute current seizure frequency when not seizure-free for ≥6 months, the best explicit rate is 2 over approximately 3 months.
